steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim states that Aristophanes satirizes sophists as having an exaggerated sense of their own wisdom. The correct quotation must show a sophist boasting about his own cleverness — not someone else praising sophists, and not a general description of their school.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Strepsiades encourages his son to learn to be a sophist, saying, "If you have any concern for your father's patrimony, become one of them."’: This shows an outsider admiring sophists, not a sophist puffing up his own wisdom. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Strepsiades says of a sophist business, "There dwell men who in speaking of the heavens persuade people that it is an oven, and that it encompasses us, and that we are the embers. These men teach, if one give them money, to conquer in speaking, right or wrong."’: A general description of the think-shop doesn't show a sophist's inflated self-regard. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Socrates, a sophist, says to a new customer, "Come, then, take care that whenever I propound any clever dogma about abstruse matters, you [seize] immediately."’: Socrates, cast as a sophist, self-importantly describes his own teachings as clever and dealing with abstruse matters — a direct display of exaggerated self-wisdom. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘Socrates, a sophist, says of a new customer, "I have not seen any man so boorish, nor so impracticable, nor so stupid, nor so forgetful; who, while learning some little petty quibbles, forgets them before he has learned them".’: Disparaging a student doesn't demonstrate the sophist's inflated view of his own wisdom. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
